rationales.security: He chased down her 'specific brand of blood pressure pills' at two pharmacies, prioritizing her health and safety despite the disruption.
rationales.conformity: He admits he was 'short with her' and heard 'her voice go small,' showing a failure to restrain irritation and preserve harmony.
rationales.benevolence: He interrupted work, drove to two pharmacies, and brought the pills home because he knew 'she's scared about her health.'

rationales.achievement: He spent 'four hours on this' even though he 'had quarterly reports due,' sidelining work obligations to handle something else.
rationales.security: He built a color-coded pill organizer after she had 'doubled up on her thyroid medication twice,' aiming for predictability and reduced risk.
rationales.benevolence: He carefully reorganized Ma's medication schedule and kept helping even while she resisted, because 'if I don't do it, who does?'

rationales.security: He frames his continued work around concrete family needs like 'tutoring fees,' 'specialist appointments,' and rent, emphasizing dependable provision.
rationales.benevolence: He says taking in Ma and funding Yuxin were 'my decisions' and that his family needing him 'is the whole point,' centering close-family care.
He gave Haojie an hour of focused help after dinner, and the moment mattered because 'nobody needed anything from me except to be there.'

rationales.achievement: He stayed through the late client dinner and KTV because Tanaka was an 'important account' and the deal 'would cover Yuxin's next semester easily.'
rationales.conformity: He says it was 'good business practice to go along, build the relationship,' showing deference to expected social-business norms.
rationales.benevolence: When Ma had a dizzy spell, he replied 'OK take care of her, back late' and later admits he 'chose convenient over present' instead of showing up for family.

rationales.achievement: The contract 'confirmed this morning,' validating the prior night's business effort and focus on securing the account.
rationales.benevolence: He recognizes Lifen 'notices when I choose work over home' and that the latest night out added to that tally, showing harm to close relationships.
He listens closely to Yuxin's struggle, advises her not to 'shoulder everything alone,' and says the family bond is the part he would 'burn everything down to keep.'

rationales.achievement: He missed the meeting because 'a shipment got held at customs,' prioritizing a business problem that needed immediate handling.
rationales.benevolence: He broke his promise to Haojie, admitting 'I told him last week I'd go this time' and that the customs excuse 'is not' good enough.
He took Haojie out for beef noodles after school and made a concrete effort to reconnect the day after letting him down.

rationales.tradition: The family independently organizes Ma's birthday with a cake, card, and special fish, sustaining a shared family ritual around honoring an elder.
rationales.benevolence: He values that everyone 'independently decides to show up for each other,' and the whole entry centers on caring for Ma together.
He decides to drive Ma to see Auntie Fang because the widow is lonely and because 'Ma will worry about Auntie Fang all week' if she cannot visit.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects staying with the proven furniture line and insists, 'I am not going to sit there manufacturing the same shelving unit for the next ten years.'
rationales.stimulation: He embraces the pivot because 'there is something there' in the urban farming kits and prefers the uncertain new direction over repeating old products.
rationales.security: He dismisses cautious objections and pushes for 'six months to prove the concept' rather than favoring the safer, established line.

rationales.hedonism: He reduced the day to a 'cheese sandwich at my desk' and tasteless leftovers, showing pleasure and comfort being sacrificed to work.
rationales.achievement: The entry is dominated by 'back-to-back calls' with suppliers and manufacturers to keep the venture moving despite exhaustion.
rationales.benevolence: He says he keeps 'throw[ing] money at the gap where my presence should be,' acknowledging that work has displaced being there for his sons.

rationales.self_direction: He resists committing to the studio lease because 'signing anything for a fixed term makes my chest tight,' showing a strong need to preserve freedom of movement.
rationales.stimulation: The whole day centers on new designs, new suppliers, and fast-moving possibilities, from sketching at 4 a.m. to chasing options in Valencia.
rationales.security: He explicitly recoils from a stable two-year lease, saying 'Two years feels like a prison sentence' and reacting against fixed predictability.

rationales.benevolence: He recognizes Matteo's acting out may trace back to when he 'cancelled the last weekend because of the Lisbon trip,' meaning he had been choosing work over his son.
He spends the weekend trying to reconnect, and the climbing wall works because it lets him and Matteo do something together until 'he forgot he was angry with me.'

rationales.achievement: He closes the Milan competition tab because he knows he would 'neglect the actual business' and wants to stay focused on revenue and the company's real needs.

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately slows down to 'just look at things,' and says 'The water helped,' showing a turn toward simple sensory and emotional relief in the present.

rationales.self_direction: He rejects the settled corporate path outright, saying he would 'rather fail at something new every two years than succeed at the same thing for twenty.'
rationales.stimulation: He defines himself by 'chasing whatever is around the next corner' and prefers novelty even after a painful resignation.
rationales.security: Pieter leaves for 'better pay, predictable hours,' while he frames himself in opposition to that stable choice and continues to reject it.
